perturbation.original_text: SUPPLIER agrees to hold all Confidential Information in strict confidence and will not disclose or use the Confidential Information for its benefit or the benefit of any other company or entity anywhere in the world or any other purpose other than for the Purpose set forth in the recitals herein.
perturbation.changed_text: SUPPLIER agrees to hold all Confidential Information in confidence and will not disclose or use the Confidential Information for its benefit or the benefit of any other company or entity anywhere in the world or any other purpose other than for the Purpose set forth in the recitals herein.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'strict' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Connecticut's Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, and diminishing the contractual language reduces the enforceability of that requirement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Conn. Gen. Stat. § 35-51 implies a standard of reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. By weakening the confidentiality clause from 'strict confidence' to simply 'confidence', the contract provides SUPPLIER with a loophole to argue that their efforts to maintain confidentiality were adequate, even if they were substandard. This undermines the protection afforded to trade secrets under Connecticut law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language requires the SUPPLIER to hold all Confidential Information in 'strict confidence,' while the changed language omits the word 'strict,' weakening the obligation. The Perturbation Explanation states that this weakens the obligation to protect confidential information and reduces the enforceability of the 'reasonable efforts' requirement of Connecticut's Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The Law Explanation elaborates, noting that this provides a loophole. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'misappropriation' and refers to a 'duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.' While Snippet 2 does not explicitly state 'strict' is required, the removal of the word 'strict' directly weakens the contractual obligation and thus creates a legal contradiction, making it easier for the SUPPLIER to argue their efforts were adequate even if substandard, thus undermining the protection afforded to trade secrets under Connecticut law.

perturbation.original_text: Any disclosure of Confidential Inform ation shall be limited SUPPLIER’s employees and who have a need to use or study such Confidential Information for the Purpose. SUPPLIER shall advise its employees and agen ts of its obligations pursuant to this Agreement, but SUPPLIER shall remain responsible to ensure their compliance.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disclosure of Confidential Inform ation shall be limited SUPPLIER’s employees who have a need to use or study such Confidential Information for the Purpose. SUPPLIER shall advise its employees and agen ts of its obligations pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'but SUPPLIER shall remain responsible to ensure their compliance' significantly reduces the SUPPLIER's liability if their employees violate the NDA. This could conflict with Connecticut agency law, which holds principals responsible for the actions of their agents within the scope of their authority.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Connecticut Law of Agency - Respondeat Superior
perturbation.law_explanation: Connecticut law (specifically the principle of Respondeat Superior codified at Conn. Gen. Stat. § 52-183) holds employers liable for the torts of their employees committed within the scope of their employment. Removing the SUPPLIER's explicit responsibility for employee compliance weakens GK's ability to hold SUPPLIER accountable for breaches of confidentiality caused by SUPPLIER's employees, thus contradicting the underlying principles of agency law in Connecticut.

Presumption of agency in motor vehicle operation. :: Chapter 899 — Evidence (contains Secs. 52-143 to 52-184c) :: Title 52 — Civil Actions (contains Chapters 895 to 929) :: 2005 Connecticut Code :: Connecticut Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Connecticut General Statutes 2024 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2023 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2022 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2005 Conn. Gen. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 Connecticut Code - Sec. 52-183. Presumption of agency in motor vehicle operation. Sec. 52-183. Presumption of agency in motor vehicle operation. In any civil action brought against the owner of a motor vehicle to recover damages for the negligent or reckless operation of the motor vehicle, the operator, if he is other than the owner of the motor vehicle, shall be presumed to be the agent and servant of the owner of the motor vehicle and operating it in the course of his employment. The defendant shall have the burden of rebutting the presumption. (1949 Rev., S. 7905; P.A. 82-160, S. 85.) History: P.A. 82-160 rephrased the section. Cited. 123 C. 396. Presumption avails plaintiff until trier finds proven the circumstances with reference to use of car and authority to drive it, leaving burden then on plaintiff to establish that car was being operated by agent in course of employment. Id., 459. Presumption not rebutted by mere offer of substantial countervailing evidence; jury not obliged to accept testimony offered by defendant on agency and may find for plaintiff even if he produces no evidence on that issue. 126 C. 587. Nature of this presumption; burden on defendant is restricted to rebutting the presumption. 130 C. 185; id., 684. Plaintiff does not waive benefit of presumption of agency by introducing evidence of the fact of agency on his case in chief. 137 C. 675. Inapplicable where there is no allegation re ownership of car. 145 C. 605. If dealer's plates are affixed to motor vehicle, they constitu
perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly states that the SUPPLIER remains responsible for ensuring their employees' compliance. The changed text removes this clause, which weakens the SUPPLIER's liability. The Law Explanation states that removing the SUPPLIER's explicit responsibility for employee compliance weakens the ability to hold SUPPLIER accountable for breaches of confidentiality caused by SUPPLIER's employees, thus contradicting the underlying principles of agency law in Connecticut. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Conn. Gen. Stat. § 52-183, establishes a presumption of agency, reinforcing the principle that employers can be held responsible for the actions of their employees within the scope of their employment, supporting the argument that removing the clause contradicts agency law.

perturbation.original_text: At any time that GKT may request, SUPPLIER shall return the Confidential Information to GK and shall certify in writing that all copies thereof in its possession have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: At any time that GKT may request, SUPPLIER shall return the Confidential Information to GK.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for the SUPPLIER to certify in writing that all copies of the confidential information have been destroyed removes a key verification measure. Connecticut's trade secret laws require companies to take active steps to protect their trade secrets. Without this certification, it's harder for GK to prove they took reasonable measures, as destroying all copies of confidential information and being able to demonstrate that fact are reasonable and commonly used protective measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, a company must actively protect its trade secrets. The certification ensures that the receiving party has indeed destroyed all copies and can prove that they no longer possess it; removing this puts the company at a legal disadvantage and it is now harder for them to enforce the CUTSA.

Gen. Stat. 2023 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2022 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2005 Conn. Gen. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 Connecticut Code - Sec. 35-51. Definitions. Sec. 35-51. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means, including searching through trash. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, including but not limited to disclosures made under section 1-210, sections 31-40j to 31-40p, inclusive, or subsection (c) of section 12-62; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, limited liability com
perturbation.justification: The omission of the certification requirement creates a legal contradiction. The original contract requires the supplier to certify in writing that all copies of the confidential information have been destroyed, providing a documented verification measure. The changed contract removes this certification requirement, which weakens the ability of GKT to prove they took reasonable measures to protect their trade secrets. According to the Law Explanation, the certification ensures that the receiving party has indeed destroyed all copies and can prove they no longer possess it. The Perturbation Explanation notes that Connecticut's trade secret laws require companies to take active steps to protect their trade secrets and that without this certification, it is harder for GK to prove they took reasonable measures. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly mention the need for certification, it defines 'misappropriation' and 'improper means', suggesting that active measures to protect trade secrets are relevant to compliance with the Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, and that removing a verification step weakens that protection.

perturbation.original_text: The parties hereby agree to submit all disputes hereunder to arbitration in Connecticut, USA in accordance with the rules of the International Chamber of Commerce . All arbitration proceedings shall be conducted exclusively in the English language before a single arbitrator. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if SUPPLIER is alleged to have breached this Agreement, then GK shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties hereby agree to submit all disputes hereunder to arbitration in Connecticut, USA in accordance with the rules of the International Chamber of Commerce . All arbitration proceedings shall be conducted exclusively in the English language before a single arbitrator.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, if SUPPLIER is alleged to have breached this Agreement, then GK shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction' forces GK to resolve all breaches through arbitration, which may be a slower and less effective remedy than seeking immediate injunctive relief to prevent further disclosure of confidential information, especially since this agreement is explicitly related to protecting Confidential Information and stopping it from being used or disclosed.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Connecticut generally favors arbitration, Conn. Gen. Stat. § 52-408 allows parties to seek judicial relief, including injunctions, even when an arbitration agreement exists, where such relief is necessary to prevent irreparable harm. Removing GK's explicit right to seek injunctive relief limits their ability to protect their trade secrets effectively, as arbitration can be time-consuming, potentially allowing further unauthorized disclosure of confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows GK to seek injunctive relief for Supplier breaches, while the changed contract removes this right, forcing all disputes into arbitration. The Law Explanation suggests that Connecticut law allows parties to seek judicial relief, like injunctions, even with an arbitration agreement, to prevent irreparable harm. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that removing GK's right to seek injunctive relief limits their ability to protect their trade secrets effectively, as arbitration can be time-consuming, potentially allowing further unauthorized disclosure of confidential information. This constitutes an Omission Legal Contradiction as it removes a clause that is important for enforcing consumer protection in cases where there is a breach.
